coredata.dc:title: Erythrocyte membrane coated with nitrogen-doped quantum dots and polydopamine composite nano-system combined with photothermal treatment of Alzheimer's disease
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Jichun
Chi, Mingyuan
Li, Lianxin
Zhang, Yuewen
Xie, Meng
coredata.dc:description: Mitochondrial dysfunction and metal ion imbalance are recognized as pathological hallmarks of Alzheimer's Disease (AD), leading to deposition of β-amyloid (Aβ) thereby and inducing neurotoxicity, activating apoptosis, eliciting oxidative stress, and ultimately leading to cognitive impairment. In this study, the red blood cell membrane (RBC) was used as a vehicle for encapsulating carbon quantum dots (CQD) and polydopamine (PDA), creating a nanocomposite (PDA-CQD/RBC). This nanocomposite was combined with near-infrared light (NIR) for AD treatment. The RBC offers anti-immunorecognition properties to evade immune clearance, PDA exhibits enzyme-mimicking activity to mitigate oxidative stress damage, and CQD acts as a chelating agent for metal ions (Cu2+), effectively preventing Cu2+-mediated aggregation of Aβ. Furthermore, the local heating induced by near-infrared laser irradiation can dismantle the formed Aβ fibers and enhance the blood–brain barrier's permeability. Both in vitro and animal experiments have shown that PDA-CQD/RBC, in combination with NIR, mitigates neuroinflammation, and ameliorates behavioral deficits in mice. This approach targets multiple pathological pathways, surpassing the limitations of single-target treatments and enhancing therapeutic efficacy while decelerating disease progression.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Erythrocyte membrane
Scavenging free radicals
Metal ion chelation
Blood-brain barrier
Photothermal therapy
Alzheimer’s disease

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Erythrocyte membranes modified of polydopamine and carbon quantum dots
Examination of inhibition of Aβ monomer aggregation and light-promoted decomposition properties of Aβ protofibrils
Measurement of intracellular reactive oxygen species
Intracellular anti-Aβ aggregation and NIR radiation for Aβ fiber depolymerization
Exploration of enzyme activities and copper ion chelating properties
Structural and morphological analysis of Aβ and Thioflavin t (ThT) fluorescence analysis
Intracellular anti- Cu2+- induced Aβ aggregates and near-infrared depolymerization of Aβ fibers
Intracellular reactive oxygen species scavenging clearance
Pathologic blood–brain barrier crossing and intracerebral distribution
In vivo fluorescence and imaging analysis
Results of amyloid plaque staining and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu province (BK20201419) and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2021 M701502 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of RBC. (b) TEM images of PDA-CQD/RBC. Particle sizes (c) and Zeta potentials (d) of PDA-CQD, RBC and PDA-CQD/RBC. (mean ± SD, n = 3). (e) Stability of PDA-CQD/RBC in four cycles (808 nm, 2 W/cm2) (f) The concentration of copper ions (grayish) and the calculated chelation rate (dark gray) in the remaining solution of PDA-CQD and PDA-CQD/RBC (mean ± SD, n = 3) (g) The variation of fluorescence intensity of PDA-CQD/RBC at 525 nm with the increase of Cu2+ concentration from 0 μM to 100 μM. After adding the materials, the UV absorption of 2, 3-dihydroxybenzoic acid (h) produced by the reaction between salicylic acid and hydroxyl radical at 510 nm, the fluorescence intensity at 425 nm of 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid (i) generated by the oxidation of ethidium hydrogen (HE) by superoxide radical (⋅ O2 –).

(a) The fluorescence intensity of ThT treated with Aβ monomers alone, Aβ monomers + Cu2+ and Aβ monomers + Cu2++PDA-CQD and PDA-CQD / RBC.(b) TEM images of Aβ oligomers alone, Aβ oligomers + Cu2+ and Aβ oligomers + Cu2++PDA-CQD/RBC incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, bar = 500 nm.(c) The fluorescence intensity of ThT treated with Aβ fibrils alone, Aβ fibrils with NIR, Aβ fibrils in the presence of PDA-CQD, PDA-CQD/RBC with/without NIR (808 nm, 2 W/cm2, 5 min).(d) TEM images of Aβ fibrils alone, Aβ fibrils + PDA-CQD and PDA-CQD/RBC (20 μg/mL) with or without NIR irradiation (808 nm,2 W/cm2, 5 min), bar = 500 nm. (e）Seven-Day fluorescence intensity changes of the amyloid-specific dye Thioflavin-t (ThT) bound to Aβ monomers after addition of PDA-CQD/RBC and of ThT to Aβ Protofibrils with and without NIR Irradiation.

Confocal laser Scanning microscope images (a) of PDA-CQD/RBC ingested by SH-SY5Y cells and relative cell viability (b) of PDA-CQD/RBC co-incubated with SH-SY5Y for 24 h (bar = 50 µm). Intracellular anti-Aβ aggregation (c) and intracellular NIR de-polymerizes Aβ fibers (d). (e)Aβ aggregation-induced DCFH-DA-ROS (Green) surge (bar = 100 µm). (mean ± SD, n = 3, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a)Morphology of SHSY-5Y cells under administration of Aβ, Aβ + PDA-CQD/RBC, and Aβ + PDA-CQD/RBC + NIR stimulation. (b)Mitochondrial membrane potential fluorescence maps. (c) PDA-CQD/RBC nanoparticles crossed the blood–brain barrier under NIR radiation, PDA-CQD/RBC was labeled with DID. (Scale bar = 100 μm).

The fluorescence image (a) in vivo with or without NIR irradiation after PBS, PDA-CQD /RBC were injected into the tail vein of mice 6 h and its corresponding fluorescence value (b). The temperature changes within 4 h (c) under NIR irradiation after PBS and PDA-CQD /RBC were injected into the tail vein of mice (3 min, 808 nm). (mean ± SD, n = 3, ***P < 0.001).

(a)Movement track of mice in each group in open field test. (b)Changes in body weight of mice in different groups and the total distance of movement (d), the relative rest time (c), the number of standing (e), the number of turning back (f), the time of staying in the central area (g) and the percentage of activity in the central area (h) of the experimental mice in the open field test. (mean ± SD, n = 5, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001).

(a) Typical swimming trajectory of mice. (b) Comparison of the escape delay time of the learning path between different groups of mice. (c)Waiting for an escape to the platform during the training phase WT mice were the control group. (d) The percentage of time spent swimming in the escape platform quadrant. (e)The number of times the target platform quadrant. (mean ± SD, n = 5, ***P < 0.001).

(a) Immunohistochemical staining of hippocampus after different treatments. (b) White boxes indicate THS staining for Aβ deposition (green). (c) HE staining in brain Quantitative histogram of brain inflammatory factor in different groups (mean ± SD, n = 5, ***P < 0.001). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic representation of the PDA-CQD/RBC nanosystem for AD combination therapy.

head.ce:title: Erythrocyte membrane coated with nitrogen-doped quantum dots and polydopamine composite nano-system combined with photothermal treatment of Alzheimer's disease

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mitochondrial dysfunction and metal ion imbalance are recognized as pathological hallmarks of Alzheimer's Disease (AD), leading to deposition of β-amyloid (Aβ) thereby and inducing neurotoxicity, activating apoptosis, eliciting oxidative stress, and ultimately leading to cognitive impairment. In this study, the red blood cell membrane (RBC) was used as a vehicle for encapsulating carbon quantum dots (CQD) and polydopamine (PDA), creating a nanocomposite (PDA-CQD/RBC). This nanocomposite was combined with near-infrared light (NIR) for AD treatment. The RBC offers anti-immunorecognition properties to evade immune clearance, PDA exhibits enzyme-mimicking activity to mitigate oxidative stress damage, and CQD acts as a chelating agent for metal ions (Cu2+), effectively preventing Cu2+-mediated aggregation of Aβ. Furthermore, the local heating induced by near-infrared laser irradiation can dismantle the formed Aβ fibers and enhance the blood–brain barrier's permeability. Both in vitro and animal experiments have shown that PDA-CQD/RBC, in combination with NIR, mitigates neuroinflammation, and ameliorates behavioral deficits in mice. This approach targets multiple pathological pathways, surpassing the limitations of single-target treatments and enhancing therapeutic efficacy while decelerating disease progression.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a complex condition with an incompletely understood pathogenesis [1]. Nevertheless, extensive research has demonstrated that the abnormal deposition of β-amyloid (Aβ) an imbalance of reactive oxygen species (ROS) contribute significantly to the development of AD. Elevated levels of metal ions, such as copper ions (Cu2+), promote the abnormal aggregation of Aβ fibers, leading to oxidative stress damage to neuronal cells [2–4], while the overproduction of ROS initiates mitochondrial dysfunction, culminating in excessive free radical production and neuroinflammatory responses [5–7]. In light of these pathological characteristics [8], a strategy that both inhibits Cu2+-induced Aβ aggregation and diminishes ROS production may prove beneficial in devising therapeutic interventions for AD, thereby preventing a detrimental cycle.

Recent advancements have been made in the development of brain-targeted drug delivery systems, with nano-sized particles (NPs) emerging as potent Aβ inhibitors [9]. Their ultra-small size enables active targeting of the blood–brain barrier (BBB) and dodges clearance by immune cells [10]. Photothermal therapy (PTT), utilizing near-infrared laser (NIR) at an 808 nm wavelength, offers the advantages of deep tissue penetration, high resolution, and superior spatial distribution. This improves BBB permeability, facilitating nanomaterials' entry into brain tissue and increasing their aggregation within the brain [11,12]. When applied to Aβ aggregates, NIR irradiation generates heat energy, increasing local temperatures to disassemble Aβ aggregates and reduce Aβ toxicity [13]. Compared to conventional AD treatments [14], such as chemotherapy and radiotherapy, combination therapy exhibits a more favorable safety profile and reduced invasiveness, demonstrating promising therapeutic outcomes in neurodegenerative diseases. Our group suggests using composite nanomaterials to target multiple etiological factors of AD in conjunction with photothermal therapy, aiming to boost treatment efficacy by simultaneously acting on multiple targets.

Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CQD) are characterized by abundant functional and water-soluble groups on their surfaces, facilitating surface modification. Nitrogen doping alters the energy band structure of CQD, enhancing their absorption and emission spectra and improving photothermal conversion efficiency [15–17]. Polydopamine (PDA) is notable for its high photothermal conversion efficiency, neuroprotective, and anti-inflammatory properties, boasting enzyme-like activity for robust antioxidant capacity to neutralize abnormal ROS [18–20]. The nitrogen-containing functional groups in PDA bind strongly to metal ions like Cu2+ through chelation, and its catechol groups exhibit potent ligand-binding capabilities [21,22]. Although PDA provides effective photothermal therapeutic effects, its solitary use as a photothermal agent necessitates high laser power, potentially harming normal cells [23,24]. Thus, the PDA-CQD composite yields a synergistic effect, endowing this nanomaterial with exceptional photothermal, metal chelating, and antioxidant properties, showing significant potential for AD therapy.

The erythrocyte membrane bionic camouflage technique offers the unique advantages of low immunogenicity, reduced nonspecific nanoparticle uptake by the reticuloendothelial system, and extended nanoparticle circulation in the blood [25–27]. Consequently, we employed the red blood cell membrane (RBC) for the camouflage of PDA-CQD (PDA-CQD/RBC), leveraging RBC to enhance the targeted delivery of PDA-CQD to diseased tissues. The metal chelating capacity of PDA-CQD/RBC efficiently sequesters excess Cu2+, prevents Aβ aggregate formation, its antioxidant ability eliminated surplus intracellular ROS, and its excellent photothermal ability facilitated Aβ protofibril depolymerization under NIR. Both in vitro and cellular experiments have demonstrated the Aβ-targeting, BBB-crossing, and ROS scavenging capabilities of PDA-CQD/RBC, ameliorating oxidative stress-induced neuronal damage. In the APP/PS1 double transgenic mouse model experiment, PDA-CQD/RBC improved behavioral responses and provided neuroprotection in the brain, enhancing memory and cognitive functions as well as learning abilities in AD mice. These findings offer valuable insights for the further development of AD treatment methodologies and materials.

Dopamine and tris solutions were acquired from Bio-service, and amyloid β peptide was sourced from Beyotime Biotechnology. FITC was procured from Sigma-Aldrich, USA, and DID dye from Dalian Meilun Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Human neuroblastoma cells (SH-SY5Y) and mouse brain microvascular endothelial cells (bEnd.3) were obtained from the Cell Bank of the Chinese Academy of Sciences. High-glucose Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) was sourced from HyClone Thermo Scientific, while South American fetal bovine serum was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific. MTT was obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. All other reagents were of analytical grade.

A sodium heparin solution of appropriate concentration (10 mg/ml) was prepared in EP tubes according to the mice's weight. The mice's orbital blood was collected, washed via centrifugation at 3800 rpm for 5 min with varying concentrations of PBS solution, discarding the supernatant and retaining the precipitate. Hemoglobin was removed through differential centrifugation at 14000 rpm for 10 min, followed by extraction of the erythrocyte membranes through an additional centrifugation step at 14,000 rpm for 10 min. The erythrocyte membranes were then purified, ultrasonically dispersed, and store at 4 ℃.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Erythrocyte membranes modified of polydopamine and carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis of polydopamine and carbon quantum dots followed established literature protocols [28,29]. Initially, dopamine (DA) underwent self-polymerization in an alkaline solution for 1 h; the solution's pH was adjusted to 4–4.5 using a hydrochloric acid solution (10 μM). The addition rate of the solution was regulated with a dropper under magnetic stirring, followed by the gradual addition of carbon dots (CQD) solution. The mixture was co-reacted for 2 h and dialyzed for 24 h to yield the PDA-CQD composite solution. The PDA-CQD was then reacted with erythrocyte membranes (RBC) at a mass ratio of 1:70 in a constant temperature shaking chamber for 24 h. The resulting solution was ultrasonically purified (200 W, 5 min) to obtain the PDA-CQD/RBC material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Examination of inhibition of Aβ monomer aggregation and light-promoted decomposition properties of Aβ protofibrils
ce:section.ce:para: Aβ fibers were prepared by incubating Aβ monomers in pH 7.4 PBS buffer at 37 °C for 7 days to facilitate self-aggregation. Using Aβ fibers as a control, the nanomaterials, both before and after erythrocyte membrane modification, were mixed with Aβ fibers and incubated for 1 h. Subsequently, the NIR group was exposed to near-infrared light (2 W/cm2, 808 nm, 5 min), followed by an additional 2-day incubation before adding THT solution. The depolymerization ability of the Aβ protofibers by the nanocomposite was assessed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The interaction between the composite nanomaterials and Aβ-Cu2+ was explored over seven days through fluorescence analysis to elucidate the nanocomposites' binding with Aβ.

The solution, prior to erythrocyte membrane modification, was exposed to a near-infrared laser (2 W/cm2, 808 nm). The temperature change was monitored using a NIR laser imager over 15 min, with readings taken every 60 s. Following the laser's deactivation, the solution's cooling process was recorded for 15 min, thus establishing a photothermal cycle.

Initially, hydroxyl radicals were produced through the Fenton reaction (0.18 mM FeCl2 and 0.1 mM H2O2), followed by their reaction with salicylic acid (SA) to form 2,3-dihydroxybenzoic acid. The nanocomposites' capacity to neutralize hydroxyl radicals was assessed by the strong UV absorption at 510 nm of the resultant 2,3-dihydroxybenzoic acid. Furthermore, ESR spectroscopy was employed to detect the characteristic DMPO-·OH peak, indicative of the interaction with the spin trapping agent DMPO-·OH. The nanomedicine's (10 µg/mL) capability to scavenge ·OH was further explored under light exposure (2 W/cm2, 5 min). Additionally, the SOD-like activity of the nanocomposites, their ability to scavenge superoxide radicals (⋅O2–), was indirectly gauged by measuring the fluorescence intensity of dihydroethidium oxide (HE) (0.5 mg/mL).

For the cytotoxicity assessment, SH-SY5Y and Bend.3 cells were seeded in 96-well plates and incubated at 5 % CO2, 37 °C for 24 h. Nanomaterials, both before and after erythrocyte membrane modification, were administered at various concentrations and incubated overnight. Cytotoxicity was evaluated using the standard MTT assay. For the cellular uptake study, DID fluorescent probe-labeled nanomaterials were introduced and co-incubated with SH-SY5Y cells. Following incubation, cells were washed with PBS to remove unbound material, fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde, and nuclei were stained with DAPI. Laser confocal microscopy was utilized to examine the cellular uptake of nanocomposites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of intracellular reactive oxygen species
ce:section.ce:para: SH-SY5Y cells, pre-seeded in six-well plates, were treated with Aβ fibers (10 µM) for one day to induce reactive oxygen species production. Post-incubation, Aβ and the culture medium were aspirated, and cells were washed with PBS. The material (50 µg/mL) was then added and co-incubated with the cells for 6 h. After discarding the medium and washing off excess material with PBS, DCFH-DA (0.1 mM) was applied for 30 min to load fluorescent probes into the cells. Unentered probes were washed away, and the fluorescence microscope was used to observe the clearance of reactive oxygen species.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular anti-Aβ aggregation and NIR radiation for Aβ fiber depolymerization
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, the materials' effect on cell viability in counteracting Aβ aggregation was assessed. Cells were cultured for 24 h, treated with materials at varying concentrations for 2 h, and subsequently co-stimulated with Aβ and copper ions for 24 h. MTT assay was conducted to evaluate the materials' intracellular resistance to Aβ aggregation before and after modification. The impact of NIR laser irradiation (1.2 W/cm2, 3 min) in promoting the disassembly of Aβ protofibrils on cell viability was also examined. Following a 2-hour incubation with nanocomposites and Aβ protofibril stimulation, the NIR group received NIR irradiation and was assessed overnight using MTT. To further investigate cellular anti-Aβ aggregation, fluorescence electron microscopy was utilized to visualize the intracellular binding of the material to Aβ.

SH-SY5Y cells were seeded in 6-well plates one day prior to the experiment, followed by the introduction of Aβ fibers and specific concentrations of materials. The light group received near-infrared light exposure and was co-incubated for two days. Cells were then treated with JC-1 dye in darkness, shielded with tin foil. After washing away the excess dye with PBS, cell culture medium was added to facilitate the observation of cell morphology and fluorescence alterations under a fluorescence microscope.

Bend.3 cells were cultured in the upper chamber of a transwell plate three days in advance, followed by seeding SH-SY5Y cells in the lower chamber once the upper chamber cells reached confluence, simulating the blood–brain barrier's in vivo conditions. Materials were mixed with DID dye at a mass ratio of 1:218 to create fluorescent nanomaterials. These fluorescent materials were added to the upper chamber of the transwell plate, and the light group received near-infrared light (1.2 W/cm2, 3 min). After two days of co-incubation, the passage of materials from the upper to the lower chamber was observed using a fluorescence microscope.

All animal experiments were approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Jiangsu University (UJS-IACUC-20220520701) and adhered to Jiangsu University's Code of Practice for Animal Experiments. DiD-labeled PDA-CQD/RBC (0.2 mg/mL) was administered intravenously to 6-week-old male Balb/c mice of standard body weight. The mice were divided into two groups: the light group was exposed to near-infrared light (808 nm, 1.2 W/cm2, 5 min) at intervals of 0.5 h, 1 h, 2 h, and 4 h post-injection, while the control group received PBS injections. Mice were euthanized 12 h after injection. The targeting of nanomedicines to the brain and the effects of light exposure were assessed by fluorescence imaging of mouse brain tissue and in vivo photothermal temperature measurements.

The spontaneous and exploratory behaviors of double transgenic APP/PS1 mice were assessed in an open field test, which evaluated the animals' autonomous behavior, exploration, and anxiety in a novel environment [15,30]. The TSE Multi Conditioning System (256060 series) tracked and analyzed various behavioral parameters.

The spatial memory and learning abilities of double transgenic APP/PS1 mice were evaluated using the Morris water maze test [15,30]. This test exploited the mice's aversion to water, forcing them to swim and locate hidden platforms, thereby assessing the potential of the drug delivery system to mitigate learning and memory impairments.

Following behavioral assessments, mice were euthanized, and brain tissues were processed for histological examination. Sections were stained to observe neuronal damage and senile plaque accumulation in the CA1 region, while HE staining revealed morphological changes associated with apoptosis in brain cells. The effect of combination therapy on the production of inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α) in mouse brain lysates was quantified using ELISA kits.

In this study, we synthesized a nanocomposite (PDA-CQD/RBC) aimed at multi-targeted combination therapy for Alzheimer's disease (AD), with the synthesis steps and anti-AD mechanism detailed in Scheme 1 . Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis revealed the vesicular morphology of the red blood cell membranes (RBC) (Fig. 1 a), and also showed successful RBC modification on the surface of PDA-CQD/RBC (Fig. 1b). As evidenced by the characteristic absorption peak of RBC at 412 nm in the UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. S1), despite PDA-CQD lacking a significant UV absorption peak within the 300–1000 nm range [31]. The composite material's average particle size, approximately 169.9 ± 2.9 nm (Fig. 1c), aligns with TEM findings and is optimal for blood–brain barrier (BBB) penetration. The zeta potential (Fig. 1d) shifted from −21.5 ± 0.6 mV for unmodified PDA-CQD to −20.3 ± 1.1 mV after RBC modification, indicating successful encapsulation. This negative zeta potential suggests high solution stability, with particle size distribution remaining stable over 7 days in a physiological environment (Fig. S2).

The BBB represents a significant obstacle in treating neurodegenerative conditions, including AD. Photothermal therapy (PTT) emerges as a promising strategy to enhance BBB permeability crucial for AD treatment. The photothermal stability of PDA-CQD/RBC was affirmed through four photothermal cycles, with no significant efficiency loss, indicating robust photothermal stability (Fig. 1e). A direct correlation between the concentration of PDA-CQD/RBC and its photothermal effect was observed, with the highest temperature reaching 49.5 ℃ at a 200 μg/ml concentration within 10 min (Fig. S3). This efficacious light-to-heat conversion, quantified at a 34.64 % conversion rate (Fig. S4), underscores PDA-CQD/RBC's potential as an effective PTT nanocarrier.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploration of enzyme activities and copper ion chelating properties
ce:section.ce:para: Copper ion (Cu2+) imbalance is a contributing factor to AD. Consequently, we examined the capacity of the composite material to chelate Cu2+. Inductively coupled plasma (ICP) analysis (Fig. 1f) revealed that the chelation rate of Cu2+ by the composite material, encapsulated with red blood cells (RBC), was 63.46 ± 0.7 %. Moreover, the analysis of fluorescence change curves at various copper ion concentrations with the materials (Fig. 1g) indicated that the composites exhibit outstanding copper ion chelating properties. The superior Cu2+ chelation capability of PDA-CQD/RBC suggests significant potential for AD treatment. Disruption in mitochondrial ROS channels impairs mitochondrial function, leading to metabolic dysregulation of free radicals and oxidative stress. Previous studies have demonstrated that polydopamine (PDA) nanomaterials exhibit enzymatic activity capable of neutralizing abnormal ROS [19,32–34]. Therefore, we assessed the enzymatic activity of the composite material in vitro. The data showed that the unmodified composite material could scavenge hydroxyl radicals (⋅OH) (Fig. 1h), while RBC-modified composite material displayed markedly enhanced scavenging efficacy. This suggests that RBC modification improves the material's water dispersion, increases surface contact, and boosts ⋅OH elimination in the solution. Furthermore, we evaluated the SOD-like enzyme activity of the composite, finding that PDA-CQD/RBC demonstrated exemplary superoxide radical (⋅O2 –) scavenging efficiency (Fig. 1i). Additionally, we conducted a detailed investigation of the composites' hydroxyl radical scavenging activity using Electron Spin Resonance (ESR) (Fig. S6). Hydroxyl radicals generated by the Fenton reaction were neutralized upon adding PDA-CQD/RBC to the solution. For the PDA-CQD/RBC + NIR group, the solution was subjected to NIR (2 W/cm2, 5 min) to assess peak changes. The ESR spectrum intensity of the DMPO⋅OH adduct served as a control. The observed decrease in peak signal after adding PDA-CQD/RBC confirmed its scavenging activity, with no noticeable increase in ESR signal amplitude after laser irradiation. These findings affirm that the PDA-CQD/RBC system can prevent the generation and accumulation of free radicals, even under light exposure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological analysis of Aβ and Thioflavin t (ThT) fluorescence analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Given Cu2+'s role in neurodegeneration through its impact on neuronal metabolism, contribution to oxidative stress and injury, and promotion of Aβ deposition and senile plaque formation, a PDA-CQD/RBC nano-capture agent has been developed to sequester free Cu2+ and inhibit Aβ aggregation, thereby offering therapeutic potential for AD. The Thioflavin T (ThT) fluorescence assay, a widely utilized technique for assessing amyloid aggregation and screening aggregation inhibitors in vitro, operates on the principle that ThT molecules exhibit minimal fluorescence in their free state but bind specifically to the β-sheets of amyloid fibers during Aβ aggregation, causing fluorescence emission. Thus, within a certain concentration range, fluorescence intensity correlates with the extent of amyloid protein aggregation, where higher fluorescence intensity signifies a greater degree of β-folded aggregates in Aβ. The ThT fluorescence assay (Fig. 2 a) enables investigation of the inhibitory effect of PDA-CQD/RBC on Aβ fibrillization. Results indicate that, compared to the control group, ThT fluorescence signal (FI) decreases when Aβ is co-cultured with Cu2+ and PDA-CQD/RBC, demonstrating that the nanomaterial binds a significant amount of Cu2+ ions and delays Aβ nucleation, thereby inhibiting Cu2+-induced Aβ aggregation. Additionally, TEM (Fig. 2b) was employed to evaluate the inhibitory effect of PDA-CQD/RBC on Aβ aggregation further. After co-culturing Aβ monomers with Cu2+ for 72 h, numerous Aβ-Cu2+ complexes with semi-fibrous and semi-granular structures were observed, aggregating into fibrous structures and intertwining, which confirms Cu2+'s role in promoting Aβ aggregation. However, the addition of PDA-CQD/RBC transformed these complexes into amorphous species, indicating that the nanomaterial can capture Cu2+ and inhibit amyloid protein aggregate formation, thereby preventing Aβ fibrillation.

The depolymerization of Aβ aggregates by PDA-CQD/RBC was further investigated using ThT fluorescence analysis. Changes in ThT fluorescence revealed a more pronounced burst in fluorescence intensity and a significant reduction in ThT FI under co-incubation conditions with composites and NIR (Fig. 2c), confirming that the photothermal effect of the composites effectively degrades and disassembles mature Aβ protofibrils, reducing their amyloidosis potential. TEM images (Fig. 2d) show that typical Aβ protofibrils, dendritic and cross-twisted in nature, maintain their long dendritic helical filamentous morphology upon NIR exposure. In contrast, administration of the composites alone results in the dissociation of Aβ protofibrils into irregular spherical structures. Under the combined influence of nanoparticles and NIR light, entangled Aβ amyloid fibril aggregates disperse into irregular fragments, forming minimal spherical aggregates, while elongated fibers nearly vanish. This validates that PDA-CQD/RBC, combined with NIR illumination, effectively disrupts Aβ aggregation and disassembles mature Aβ fibrils, with NIR irradiation causing irreversible denaturation of Aβ amyloid protein.

The ThT fluorescence assay quantifies Cu2+-enhanced amyloid fibrillation and monitors the Aβ aggregation process to form amyloid fibrils (Fig. 2e). Fluorescence intensity changes measured over 7 consecutive days were utilized to track ThT fluorescence intensity of aggregates during Aβ aggregation, with data normalization providing kinetic curves of Aβ aggregation. With extended incubation time, compared to treatment with Aβ - Cu2+ alone, the combination of the composite material and light treatment significantly hinders the mutual aggregation of Aβ monomers, decelerates the catalytic nucleation process of Aβ fibers, and reduces oligomer formation.

These experimental findings demonstrate that PDA-CQD/RBC effectively reduces the interaction between Cu2+ and Aβ under NIR light, preventing Cu2+-mediated Aβ aggregation. It intervenes in the aggregation and deposition process of Aβ proteins, effectively reducing Aβ-induced toxicity.

To evaluate the uptake of PDA-CQD/RBC by SH-SY5Y cells, we labeled PDA-CQD/RBC with a cell membrane infrared fluorescent probe (DID), resulting in red fluorescence within the cells. The nuclear dye (DAPI) was utilized to stain the cell nucleus, producing blue fluorescence. Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) was employed to monitor the cellular uptake of the fluorescent materials. Fig. 3 a demonstrates that after 2 h of co-incubation with SH-SY5Y cells, PDA-CQD/RBC accumulated significantly in the perinuclear region, with fluorescence primarily localized in the cytoplasm. This confirms the successful cellular uptake of PDA-CQD/RBC, indicating its cytoplasmic localization.

The biosafety of PDA-CQD/RBC was assessed by examining the viability of SH-SY5Y cells and Bend.3 cells treated with PDA-CQD/RBC using a standard MTT assay (Fig. 3b, S4). The results indicated that neither PDA-CQD nor PDA-CQD/RBC triggered significant apoptosis, maintaining cell viability consistently above 90 %, which suggests low cellular toxicity. Interestingly, the encapsulation of RBC reduced cytotoxicity, highlighting the high biosafety of the nanocomposites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular anti- Cu2+- induced Aβ aggregates and near-infrared depolymerization of Aβ fibers
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of Aβ aggregation's final products, induced by copper ions, was evaluated using the MTT assay to examine the inhibitory effect of PDA-CQD/RBC on Aβ aggregation. A concentration of 10 μM Aβ together with 20 μg/ml Cu2+ was selected to determine whether the nanomaterials could inhibit Aβ aggregation at the cellular level, thereby reducing Aβ-induced toxicity. Fig. 3c showcases the intracellular anti-Aβ aggregation effect of PDA-CQD/RBC.

Results revealed that, compared to the control group, Cu2+ enhanced Aβ fibrillation, leading to increased cell toxicity. However, co-incubation of cells with PDA-CQD/RBC markedly improved cell viability in a concentration-dependent manner. The cell survival rate dropped to 35.7 ± 0.4 % with Aβ and Cu2+ alone, whereas it rose to 88.2 ± 3.2 % at a concentration of 50 μg/ml PDA-CQD/RBC. This demonstrates that PDA-CQD/RBC enhances cellular Aβ clearance, reducing Aβ oligomer-induced neurotoxicity and promoting the clearance of Aβ aggregates across a concentration range of 10–50 μg/ml. PDA-CQD/RBC interfered with the pathway of Cu2+-induced Aβ aggregation and degraded Aβ fibrils induced by Cu2+, thereby reducing neuronal damage caused by Aβ aggregates and significantly lowering the cytotoxicity of Aβ aggregation's final products.

Given the excellent photothermal properties of PDA-CQD/RBC in vitro (Fig. 3d), the intracellular dissociation of mature Aβ fibers by PDA-CQD/RBC nanoparticles was investigated through cell viability. Cell viability analysis was performed on cells treated with both laser irradiation and nanoparticles to quantify the cell-protective effect of PDA-CQD/RBC nanoparticles under laser irradiation accurately. Laser irradiation alone did not significantly affect SH-SY5Y cell activity, but its combination with PDA-CQD/RBC significantly mitigated apoptosis. This suggests that under NIR light stimulation, the photothermal effects increase cell membrane permeability, enhancing the cellular uptake of PDA-CQD/RBC. Moreover, NIR light can directly dissociate Aβ fibers, meaning PDA-CQD/RBC + NIR effectively reduces Aβ fiber-induced cytotoxicity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular reactive oxygen species scavenging clearance
ce:section.ce:para: PDA can engage in intracellular redox reactions via the antioxidant pathway, neutralizing various free radicals, diminishing the abnormal production and accumulation of ROS, and effectively shielding neuronal cells from oxidative stress damage prompted by Aβ-stimulation sources. SH-SY5Y cells were pre-treated with Aβ to simulate oxidative stress conditions, and green fluorescence of ROS was observed in DCFH-DA labeled cells using fluorescence microscopy (Fig. 3e). Intracellular ROS production was notably increased by Aβ stimulation alone. It was hypothesized that the uptake of PDA-CQD/RBC might directly suppress intracellular ROS production, with erythrocyte membrane modification augmenting the inhibitory effect. Additionally, PDA-CQD/RBC might adsorb Aβ, reducing Aβ aggregation and consequently curbing the ROS surge induced by intracellular Aβ deposition. The presence of PDA-CQD/RBC effectively countered Aβ aggregation and reduced cellular basal ROS levels to protect the cells from neurotoxic invasion. PDA-CQD/RBC acts as a high-performance ROS scavenger for neuroprotection and as an Aβ scavenger to mitigate ROS emergence due to Aβ deposition, possessing potential to activate the body's antioxidant defense system.

The impact of nanomaterials on the morphology of normal cells is a critical aspect of evaluating nanomaterials' biosafety. SH-SY5Y cells were chosen as the model and examined using electron microscopy. As depicted in Fig. 4 a, SH-SY5Y cells treated with Aβ fibers alone exhibited cell membrane ruffling and severe atrophy, appearing in a state suggestive of cell death, which highlighted the strong cytotoxicity of Aβ fibers. These damages were effectively mitigated in cells treated with PDA-CQD/RBC, where cell synapses and branches showed noticeable recovery and normal growth. Conversely, cells co-treated with PDA-CQD/RBC + NIR maintained morphology comparable to the blank control group, with no evident damage to the cytoskeleton, indicating that PDA-CQD/RBC + NIR not only mitigated Aβ cytotoxicity but also preserved normal cell morphology, offering neuroprotective effects.

Mounting evidence suggests that Aβ fibrils' cytotoxicity stems not only from excessive ROS production but also from mitochondrial damage. JC-1 dye was utilized to assess the mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) under various treatment conditions, to determine if cell apoptosis induced by Aβ fibers could be alleviated by PDA-CQD/RBC. In healthy mitochondria, high MMP allows JC-1 to accumulate within mitochondria, forming aggregates that emit red fluorescence. When mitochondria are compromised, MMP decreases, causing JC-1 to exist as monomers outside the mitochondria, emitting green fluorescence. The experimental results (Fig. 4b) revealed a decrease in monomers and an increase in aggregates compared to the control group, indicating a increased cellular MMP. Treatment with PDA-CQD/RBC + NIR significantly elevated the number of orange signals compared to the Aβ group, suggesting an improvement in MMP damaged by Aβ, thereby protecting the cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pathologic blood–brain barrier crossing and intracerebral distribution
ce:section.ce:para: The tight junctions and efflux transport characteristics of the blood–brain barrier (BBB) typically impede the passage of substances. For nanosystems aimed at treating AD, the ability to cross the BBB is crucial. To assess the permeability of the leading PDA-CQD/RBC nanomaterials through the BBB, Bend.3 and SH-SY5Y cells were employed to model the BBB in vitro. Fluorescence microscopy examined the uptake of nanoparticles by the SH-SY5Y cells beneath (Fig. 4c). The passage of PDA-CQD/RBC, labeled with DiD, through the BBB was inferred from the fluorescence signal intensity. The PDA-CQD/RBC-treated group exhibited weak fluorescence signals in the lower chamber of the transwell-plate. However, aiming to minimize near-infrared light's neuronal damage, a lower laser power was selected for irradiation. After NIR light irradiation, the red signals considerably intensified, indicating the material's ability to infiltrate the lower chamber of the transwell-plate. Under 808 nm laser irradiation, cell membrane permeabilization by PDA-CQD/RBC was affected by temperature. The photothermal effects' thermal effect can enhance the BBB's permeability. PDA-CQD/RBC nanoparticles demonstrate robust transport capacity and cellular uptake. Under NIR light, PDA-CQD/RBC could effectively traverse the BBB and enter neuronal cells, boosting neuronal cell uptake.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo fluorescence and imaging analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the targeted delivery of RBC-modified PDA-CQD nanocarriers, whole-body fluorescence imaging tracked the distribution and targeting capability of the nanocarriers. Biomimetic modification promotes nanomaterial accumulation in the mouse brain [35–37]. DiD-labeled PDA-CQD/RBC NPs were intravenously injected into Balb/c mice, and an in vivo fluorescence imaging system quantitatively assessed the fluorescence intensity and nanomaterial accumulation in the mouse brain. Twelve hours post-injection of PDA-CQD/RBC, mice in the PDA-CQD/RBC + NIR group showed widespread and intense fluorescence distribution in the brain (Fig. 5 a). Quantitative analysis of fluorescence intensity revealed that the brain fluorescence intensity in the PDA-CQD/RBC + NIR group was approximately 1.38 times higher than in the non-irradiated group (Fig. 5b). A near-infrared thermal imager recorded temperature changes in the brains of PBS mice and those injected with the nanomaterial over 4 h (Fig. 5c), indicating that the PDA-CQD/RBC nanosystem induced a more significant temperature difference in the mouse brain compared to the control group. These experimental results suggest that after NIR light irradiation, the fluorescence signal in the mouse brain significantly increased, demonstrating that near-infrared light enhances the efficient penetration of PDA-CQD/RBC through the BBB and its accumulation in the brain. Intense fluorescence was observed in the mouse brain even 12 h later, highlighting the nanoparticles' distribution in brain tissue and their prolonged circulation characteristics.

The open field test (OFT) is utilized to assess the spontaneous locomotor activity of mice, specifically observing the exploratory behavior of APP/PS1 mice in an unfamiliar environment through the open field system. Various parameters are recorded to evaluate their level of self-attention and environmental vigilance. Mice exhibiting excessive anxiety and nervousness tend to display less random exploratory behavior in the central area of the new environment. Mice displaying lower levels of anxiety are inclined to spend more time in the center of the open field, i.e., the open area. The experiment demonstrated that the weight of the mice did not significantly fluctuate (Fig. 6 b), indicating that the injected drugs and near-infrared light treatment did not cause significant harm to the mice. The movement patterns of different mouse groups in the minefield experiments (Fig. 6a) reveal that mice in the combined treatment group exhibited increased mobility and enhanced exploration compared to AD mice. According to the experimental data (Fig. 6c-h), it was observed that, compared to the model group, the total distance traveled and escape time for mice in the combined treatment group significantly increased, signifying an enhancement in motor activities. Specifically, the PDA-CQD/RBC + NIR group showed an increase in time and distance moved in the center area, suggesting an elevated exploratory drive. Thus, the mobility of mice in the combined treatment group was improved. These results indicate that PDA-CQD/RBC ameliorated anxiety-like behavior, enhanced locomotor activity, and increased exploratory behavior in AD mice.

The Morris Water Maze experiment was conducted to determine if the treatment material could enhance the spatial learning ability and memory cognition of APP/PS1 mice. Following the injection cycle (Fig. 7 a), it was visually apparent that co-treated mice exhibited stronger locomotor characteristics, were able to identify and remain in the target quadrant where the platform was located, and traversed the platform more frequently. The 5-day training escape time histogram (Fig. 7b–e) showed that escape latency for each mouse group gradually decreased with training, with the speed at which the joint treatment group mice reached the platform significantly improved, indicating enhanced memory in the joint treatment group. Based on additional experimental data plots, mice in the combined treatment group searched for the platform in a shorter time and stayed in the target quadrant longer, demonstrating that PDA-CQD/RBC and PDA-CQD/RBC + NIR variably restored the memory and learning abilities of AD model mice, potentially rescuing memory loss and repairing motor impairments in AD mice.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results of amyloid plaque staining and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay
ce:section.ce:para: Following the behavioral assessment, mice were euthanized to assess the impact of six weeks of PDA-CQD/RBC administration on mouse brain health. Hematoxylin and eosin (HE) staining (Fig. 8 a) evaluated the morphological structure and distribution of hippocampal neurons. No significant pathological changes were observed in the brains post-administration, with hippocampal neurons appearing clear, well-organized, and structurally intact. There was no evidence of neuronal loss, fragmentation, or apoptosis, nor were there signs of disrupted or disorganized neuronal arrangement. These findings suggest that PDA-CQD/RBC does not adversely affect the mouse brain, indirectly confirming its high biocompatibility.

The accumulation and distribution of Aβ in the brains were examined through immunofluorescence using thapsigargin S staining (Fig. 8b). Results indicated a high level of Aβ plaque expression in the mesocortex and hippocampus of AD mice, evidenced by increased green fluorescence. Conversely, the presence of Aβ-stained plaques in the brains of mice treated with PDA-CQD/RBC + NIR was significantly reduced compared to the AD group, suggesting that injection of APP/PS1 mice with PDA-CQD/RBC via the tail vein, combined with photothermal therapy, effectively reduces the number and area of Aβ plaques in the brain.

Inflammation is a key risk factor for AD, with cellular inflammatory cytokines playing a role in AD pathology. TNF-α and Aβ are associated with learning and memory deficits in AD, while IL-1β and IL-6 can trigger inflammatory responses and promote the proliferation and activation of inflammatory cells, contributing to the deposition of beta-amyloid. Quantitative analysis using enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) kits demonstrated that PDA-CQD/RBC + NIR treatment significantly reduced the levels of inflammatory factors (TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6) in the cortex and hippocampus of AD mice, as shown in the bar graphs for each group (Fig. 8c). These results indicate that the treatment alleviated oxidative stress and neuroinflammation in the brains of AD mice.

In this study, we successfully developed a small-sized nano-composite exhibiting excellent photothermal conversion properties, strong affinity for Aβ, and multispecies enzyme activity and combined it with photothermal therapy for multi-targeted treatment of Alzheimer's disease, which overcame the limitations associated with solo photothermal therapy and treatments using quantum dot materials alone, with favorable results. CQD function as metal chelating agents, capturing excess Cu2+ to inhibit Aβ aggregation. Simultaneously, the enzyme-like activity of PDA eliminates free radicals and reactive oxygen species in mitochondrial metabolism, reducing oxidative stress incidence. Moreover, the local heat generated by NIR light facilitates the dissolution of existing Aβ fibril precipitates. Compared with the existing literature [38–40], erythrocyte membrane biomimetic modification technology decreases the recognition and phagocytosis of PDA-CQD/RBC by immune cells in vivo, thus evading immune clearance and achieving prolonged circulation in vivo. The photothermal properties of PDA-CQD/RBC enable it to effectively cross the blood–brain barrier and target the lesion site more precisely, aided by NIR and the inflammation-targeting capabilities of the erythrocyte membrane. Under NIR irradiation, irreversible denaturation of Aβ reduces its toxicity. Furthermore, the enzyme-like properties of PDA-CQD/RBC facilitate the removal of abnormal free radicals and reactive oxygen species. In vitro experiments demonstrated that PDA-CQD/RBC possesses outstanding anti-Aβ deposition and ROS elimination capabilities, effectively integrating with NIR photothermal ablation of Aβ fibrils. Cellular experiments confirmed the composite's safety and its significant reduction in cell viability loss due to Aβ toxicity. Animal behavioral experiments and immunohistochemical analyses revealed that the combination of phototherapy and nanocomposites could enhance the cognitive functions of AD mice by inhibiting Aβ monomer aggregation to decrease the formation and accumulation of amyloid plaques, disintegrating Aβ fibrils, reducing their accumulation in the brain, and scavenging ROS to foster the regeneration of neuronal cells and synaptic connectivity. In order to fully understand the therapeutic promise of PDA-CQD/RBC in vivo, further exploration of the long-term distribution, in vivo accumulation and metabolism of PDA-CQD/RBC in vivo is therefore required. In summary, the multi-targeted synergistic nano-system combined with photothermal therapy proposed in this study holds significant potential for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease, promising to advance research on neurological disorders, deepen understanding of their mechanisms, and aid in developing novel therapeutic strategies.

Jichun Liu: Writing – original draft, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation. Mingyuan Chi: Project administration, Formal analysis, Data curation. Lianxin Li: Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis. Yuewen Zhang: Project administration, Formal analysis, Data curation. Meng Xie: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu province (BK20201419) and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2021M701502).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2024.02.219.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biomimetic anisotropic polymeric nanoparticles coated with red blood cell membranes for enhanced circulation and toxin removal
Sci. Adv.
